text: Upon initial laparoscopic entry into the abdomen, the surgeon observes a right-sided spleen, midline liver, and right-sided stomach. The gallbladder is visible, and extensive peritoneal attachments are noted. Ladd's bands are identified between the loops of small bowel and at the base of the gallbladder.

The surgeon observes engorged and dilated large vessels on the right side of the screen, with enlarged loops of small bowel visible in the left upper quadrant. The small bowel is seen diving posterior and inferior to the mesentery. Electrocautery is used to perform extensive adhesiolysis to mobilize the duodenum, small bowel, and explore the base of the liver.

The surgeon examines the inferior wall of the gallbladder, revealing additional abnormal anatomy. A vascular structure, identified as a pre-duodenal portal vein, is visible in the center of the screen. The duodenum is seen coursing posterior to this vein and extending into the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon continues dissection to take down peritoneal attachments and LADS bands, revealing engorged vessels and kinking of the small bowel. As the attachments are reduced, the duodenum and small bowel are straightened into a more anatomically favorable position, reducing obstruction. The portal vein becomes more apparent during the dissection.

The video shows extensive vascular congestion with the portal vein visible on the left, engorged vessels on the right, and the liver at the top of the screen. The surgeon performs hand-over-hand reduction of the small bowel from the posterior mesentery, identifying LADS bands attaching the small bowel to the cecum. These bands are reduced, exposing the appendix, which is non-inflamed and located in the right lower quadrant.

The surgeon extracorporealizes the appendix and performs suture ligation. The appendix is located in the right lower quadrant, and an appendectomy is considered due to the patient's history of malrotation. Electrocardia is used to seal the opening of the appendix, concluding the procedure.
